answer: Both Nāradasmṛti and Bṛhaspati Smṛti share the procedural rule for administering varna-differentiated oaths to witnesses. Nārada states the full four-varna formula in Nar. 1.181 (work slug: unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2, chapter 1, paragraph p-7eb699): 'One should make a Brāhmaṇa swear by truth, a Kṣatriya by his vehicle and weapons, a Vaiśya by his cattle, seeds, and gold, and a Śūdra by all sins.' Bṛhaspati echoes the opening of this formula in Brh_1,19.32 (work slug: unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070, chapter 1, paragraph p-838264): 'He should make a Brahmin swear by truth, a Kshatriya by his vehicle and weapons.' The first half of the formula — Brahmin by truth, Kshatriya by vehicle and weapons — is verbatim the same in both texts, confirming Bṛhaspati's knowledge of Nārada's rule. However, Bṛhaspati's version reads as a departure on two counts: (1) it truncates the enumeration entirely, dropping the Vaishya and Shudra formulas with no substitution; and (2) it embeds the rule narrowly within the boundary-dispute section (Brh_1.19), preceded by Brh_1,19.31 ('Sworn by their own oaths, they should state the decision on the boundary…'), which reframes the oath as testimony by boundary witnesses/arbiters rather than the general witness-examination procedure Nārada describes. The omission of Vaishya and Shudra appears intentional rather than accidental — boundary disputes in dharmaśāstra typically involve Brahmin and Kshatriya landholders — making this a contextual narrowing of Nārada's universal rule. The result is best characterized as a departure: Bṛhaspati retains the kernel of Nārada's formula but strips it of its generality and silently drops two of the four varnas.
